questions.question: Explain the difference between maskable and non-maskable interrupts with examples of each.
keyPoints: Maskable interrupts can be enabled/disabled by the CPU using control registers
Non-maskable interrupts (NMI) cannot be disabled and are for critical events
Examples: Keyboard, timer, disk I/O are maskable; power failure, hardware malfunction are NMI

questions.question: Describe the sequence of operations that occur when an interrupt is triggered.
keyPoints: Device raises interrupt request on IRQ line
CPU finishes current instruction being executed
CPU acknowledges interrupt via INTA signal
CPU saves current state (PC, status registers)
CPU fetches interrupt vector from device or controller
CPU executes the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)
ISR completes and CPU restores saved state
CPU returns to main program execution

questions.question: Compare vectored interrupts with polled interrupts. Give one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Vectored: Device provides ISR address directly - faster response but more hardware
Polled: CPU checks each device sequentially - simpler hardware but slower
Vectored advantage: Lower interrupt latency
Polled disadvantage: Wastes CPU cycles checking idle devices

questions.question: What is an interrupt vector table? How does the CPU use it during interrupt handling?
keyPoints: Table storing memory addresses of interrupt service routines
Each interrupt type has a unique entry in the table
CPU uses interrupt type number as index to fetch the corresponding vector
Vector contains starting address of the appropriate ISR

questions.question: Explain the daisy chain interrupt priority method with a diagram description.
keyPoints: Devices connected in series forming a chain
Each device has interrupt request and acknowledge lines
Priority determined by position in chain - closer to CPU = higher priority
When interrupt acknowledged, first device in chain with request responds
Earlier devices can block later ones by not propagating acknowledge signal

questions.question: What is interrupt latency? Explain two factors that affect interrupt latency in a computer system.
keyPoints: Interrupt latency is time between interrupt request and start of ISR execution
Factors: 1) Current CPU instruction completion time
2) Priority of current task being executed
3) Interrupt controller response time
4) ISR vector fetch time

questions.question: Differentiate between edge-triggered and level-triggered interrupts.
keyPoints: Edge-triggered: Activated by signal transition (change from low to high)
Level-triggered: Activated by maintaining active voltage level
Edge-triggered: Less susceptible to noise but may miss sustained signals
Level-triggered: Can cause repeated interrupts if signal stays active

questions.question: Explain the working of an interrupt controller (PIC) in managing multiple interrupt requests. Include details about priority resolution and vector generation.
keyPoints: PIC receives multiple IRQ requests from different devices
Maintains a priority scheme to determine which request to service first
Can mask individual interrupt requests
When CPU acknowledges interrupt, PIC provides interrupt vector
Modern PICs (APIC) support multiple processors and more interrupts
Handles nesting, edge/level triggered interrupts appropriately

questions.question: Discuss the concept of interrupt nesting. Explain how priority is maintained during nested interrupts and what safeguards are needed.
keyPoints: Allows higher priority interrupts to preempt lower priority ISRs
Requires saving and restoring processor state at each nesting level
Priority hardware must distinguish between interrupt levels
Stack must be large enough to hold multiple saved states
Improves system responsiveness for critical time-sensitive events
Without proper nesting, critical interrupts could be delayed

questions.question: Design and explain an interrupt-based I/O system. Compare its efficiency with programmed I/O (polling) in terms of CPU utilization.
keyPoints: Interrupt-based: CPU initiates I/O and continues other work, device interrupts when done
Programmed I/O: CPU polls device continuously, waiting for completion
Interrupt advantages: Better CPU utilization, parallel operation, faster response to events
Interrupt disadvantages: More complex hardware and software
For slow devices with long I/O times, interrupt saves many CPU cycles
For very fast devices, overhead of interrupt handling may be significant

questions.question: Explain the different types of interrupts in detail with respect to their hardware implementation and use cases.
keyPoints: Hardware interrupts: From external devices (keyboard, mouse, timer, disk)
Software interrupts: From CPU instructions (INT n, syscall)
Maskable vs Non-maskable: Based on whether CPU can ignore them
Vectored vs Polled: Based on how CPU identifies the source
Level vs Edge triggered: Based on interrupt signal detection method
Internal vs External: Based on source origin
